data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Harness strictly beats self-consistency iff the critic fixes more minority-fragile wrong-plurality questions than it breaks majority-fragile right-plurality questions; in measured data it tied because base error was so low that almost all questions were super-majority-correct, so neither fix nor break events could occur.", "mechanism": "Let M be the set of questions where the plurality answer is correct and its margin is < k/2 (majority-fragile). Let W be the set where plurality is wrong and the correct-answer block plus the critic-flagged block would form a new plurality. Harness beats SC iff |critic correctly flags W \u2296 new-plurality-flips-correct| > |critic false-flags M \u2296 plurality-flips-wrong|. At measured base error ~0.03 (easy) and ~0.01 (hard), P(majority-fragile) is tiny, so both terms are near zero and the difference cannot be positive with statistical significance. Strict gain requires base error in a mid-zone (roughly 1/(k+1) < e < 1/2) so that enough questions sit in M and W for the critic's precision/recall to create a nonzero fix-minus-break margin.", "scope": {"covers": ["k=5 with base error in [0.08, 0.30]", "critic precision p in [0.3, 0.7] and recall r in [0.3, 0.8]", "questions with genuine candidate disagreement (not ceiling items)"], "excludes": ["base error < 0.05 (super-majority regime): no fragile majorities to break or fix", "base error > 0.5 (plurality almost always wrong): SC already fails, harness needs near-perfect critic", "critic precision < 0.3 at any recall: false flags dominate breaks"], "forbidden": [{"case": "Experiment shows harness accuracy lower than SC accuracy at any tested base-error point in [0.08, 0.30] with critic precision >= 0.4", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "Measured fix-minus-break margin is <= 0 at k=7 and base error ~0.20 across >= 400 questions", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "80% confidence interval for harness-minus-SC accuracy difference includes zero at every tested point", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}], "prose_notes": "The measured v4-pro and v4-flash datasets are at the extremes: easy set at ceiling, hard set with base error 0.01 and 4/5 correct majorities. Neither can show strict harness gain because fragile-majority events are too rare. The decisive experiment must synthesize a mid-zone base error by selecting questions where the generator is known to err at moderate rates. Prediction: harness gain is a tent function of base error, peaking near e = 1/3 for k=5. The experiment must measure critic precision and recall per condition and compute fix-minus-break directly, not just end-to-end accuracy."}
